welcome: Nothing on this page is invented. Every story below traces to a record with a date on it — a ship manifest, a parish register, a census sheet, a courthouse file — and where something is still a lead rather than a fact, it says so.

hero.title: The village under the lake
hero.body: Zarges family memory says the grandfather worked a titled family's estate in Germany — tending sheep and baking bread in a big outdoor oven for the household — and that after the family sailed for America, their village of Basdorf was flooded for a reservoir. The records are starting to catch up with the memory: Zarges births cluster around Basdorf and Helsen, near Bad Arolsen — the seat of the Princes of Waldeck — and the Eder valley just south of there was dammed and flooded between 1908 and 1914. The lake is still there. Whether the family's Basdorf lies under it is one of the questions we are working.

journey.title: From a Baltic port to a Pennsylvania coal town
journey.body: In March 1910, John Romejko Sr. left the parish country northeast of Vilnius and boarded the steamship Lituania at Libau, on the Baltic coast of what is now Latvia. The manifest lists him as Iwan Romeiko, nineteen years old. He landed at Ellis Island on 30 March and went where the work was: Shenandoah, Pennsylvania — anthracite country — where his naturalization papers place him on South Cotton Street by 1913.

route.note: the parish country we are searching, northeast of Vilnius

timeline.label: Thomas Stranford and Mary Rogers born in Ireland
John Stranford born in County Londonderry

John crosses on the Lituania, Libau to New York

facts.tag: The oldest name on the tree
facts.title: The trail ends, for now, in Ulster
facts.body: The deepest documented root is John Stranford — born March 1795 in County Londonderry, Ireland, died 1855 in Venango County, Pennsylvania. His parents, Thomas Stranford and Mary Rogers, born around 1770, sit at the current edge of the line. Getting past them means Ulster parish registers, not American records — that is the next climb.

facts.title: Sworn in by the saloon keeper
facts.body: Citizenship took two witnesses willing to swear to your character. In June 1916, John Romejko Sr. brought John Dzienis, who kept a saloon at 121 South Main Street, and a neighbor, Peter Alenaucz. No lawyers. In a town like Shenandoah, the man whose word carried weight was the one behind the bar.

facts.title: The house on West Lloyd Street
facts.body: Jonathan Romejko died in 1935; his wife Josephine in 1938. Their son Joseph, still a schoolboy, went to his grandmother Antoinette at 210 West Lloyd Street — the 1940 census taker wrote him down as her 'nephew'; the 1950 census corrected it to 'grandson.' From that address he registered for the wartime draft, and in 1949 the Republican & Herald printed his Penn State graduation notice. Home address in the paper: still 210 West Lloyd.

facts.title: The Korsak name in the armorials
facts.body: Korsak is a documented szlachta (noble) clan in Boniecki's armorial of Polish-Lithuanian gentry, with recorded members in the Vilnius lands — the same region where Alexander Korsack swore he was born. The geography fits; the lineage is unproven. Until a parish record ties our Korsacks to the clan, this stays a lead, not an ancestor.

region.name: Buivydžiai, Lithuania
region.blurb: The Romejko and Korsack lines trace back toward this little parish town (Polish: Bujwidze), northeast of Vilnius, in what was then the Russian Empire. We are searching its 19th-century church registers page by page for the family's baptisms.
region.image_caption: A page from the 1890 Buivydžiai parish baptism register (Lithuanian State Historical Archives, Fund 1399) — the kind of record we read line by line, in Cyrillic script.
